title: Forelimb reaching-grasping task
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"8esrc","text":"The forelimb reaching-grasping task is a widely used behavioral assay to assess sensorimotor function in rodent models. This protocol outlines a standardized approach to training and evaluating rodents in a reaching and grasping task, aiming to ensure consistency and reliability across studies. The task involves training freely moving rodents to reach through an aperture to grasp a drop of water, allowing for the assessment of fine motor skills and coordination. Notably, the reaching movement executed in this task is not naturally present in rodent behavior and requires dedicated training for successful performance. This protocol provides detailed guidelines on apparatus setup, training schedules, and data collection methods.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"4884q","text":"1. Animal preparation","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":21}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2pi10","text":"Rodents are housed under controlled environmental conditions, including a 12-hour light/dark cycle.\nMild water restriction is applied to encourage task engagement, ensuring that each animal receives a minimum of 1 mL of water per day to maintain proper hydration and welfare.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"6d4d9","text":"To minimize stress and facilitate task learning, animals undergo a habituation period before training, allowing them to acclimate to both handling and the experimental apparatus.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"56nao","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4t5ti","text":"2. Training protocol","type":"header-three","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":20}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"7pgn4","text":"The training regimen begins with a three-day habituation phase (15-minute sessions per day), during which the water spout is positioned flush against the chamber wall (0 cm) at a height of 5.5 cm from the floor. This initial setup allows animals to associate the spout with the water reward.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"8t063","text":"Following habituation, the spout is incrementally shifted 1 cm away from the wall while maintaining the same height. This adjustment requires the mice to extend their forelimbs to obtain the water drop, reinforcing the reaching behavior.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"7ek6l","text":"Once mice consistently use their forelimbs to retrieve the water reward, formal training begins. Training continues for a minimum of four consecutive days, with daily 15-minute sessions.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"9djgm","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"brugm","text":"3. Welfare monitoring","type":"header-three","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":21}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3tmld","text":"To ensure animal welfare, daily body weight measurements must be recorded. If an animal's weight drops below 90-85% of its baseline, both water restriction and task participation should be immediately suspended to prevent excessive stress or dehydration.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"45df7","text":"Additionally, general health and behavior should be monitored to detect signs of distress or adverse effects from training.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3hm8i","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"8vqcd","text":"4. Performance assessment","type":"header-three","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":25}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3eu13","text":"A trial is considered successful if the mouse extends its forelimb, makes contact with the water drop, and successfully consumes it.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3n53l","text":"Key performance metrics include:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"6ttuk","text":"Success rate (percentage of successful trials)","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":14,"length":31}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bm36b","text":"Number of reaching attempts per session","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"a62bs","text":"Kinematic analysis of reaching movements","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"936rv","text":"These parameters should be assessed across multiple days to evaluate sensorimotor learning and adaptation.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"67nph","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"frtvl","text":"5. Considerations ","type":"header-three","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":18}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"13na8","text":"If a mouse exhibits side bias (consistent preference for one forelimb), access to the preferred limb can be restricted using a physical barrier near the narrow aperture on the opposite side.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"f2254","text":"The setup allows for video recording of reaching behavior, as well as optogenetic inhibition or excitation (via opsin expression) to assess the neural mechanisms underlying forelimb control.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"a1edl","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"1vemv","text":"6. Video Analysis","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":17}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"9q9qf","text":"Include a reference marker in the video frame (e.g., a ruler or a known-distance object) to enable conversion from pixels to centimeters for kinematic analysis.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"920lv","text":"Video analysis can be performed manually or with automated tracking tools to extract detailed kinematic parameters:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2urmq","text":"Manual annotation can be done using software such as Kinovea, which allows frame-by-frame assessment of movement execution.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"6k50r","text":"Automated kinematic tracking can be achieved using DeepLabCut or similar deep-learning-based approaches to track body points, enabling precise movement quantification.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"5ja3g","text":"These analyses help to extract fine-grained motor features such as velocity, trajectory, and limb coordination, providing a more comprehensive assessment of motor function and learning.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"4i7bk","text":"Start water restriction at least 24 hours before the first session.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"avrt8","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
materials_text: {"blocks":[{"key":"rk43","text":"The task is conducted in a custom-built transparent chamber (10 x 10 cm) with a front wall containing a narrow aperture through which the rodent can reach/grasp.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"6hfmj","text":"A small spout positioned just outside the aperture delivers a drop of water each 7 seconds. The spout should be adjustable to ensure proper positioning relative to the testing phase (habituation or training).","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bbmte","text":"The floor should provide stable footing to avoid postural instability during task execution.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"5n50o","text":"Mice behavior was recorded daily using a FLIX camera (60 fps, 800 × 800 px) positioned laterally to capture detailed movement kinematics.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Determine the baseline weight of the mice through initial measurements.

Start recording and water drop delivery Begin continuous video recording to monitor behavior. Automatically or manually dispense a water drop at the spout at regular intervals to familiarize mice with the reward location. Observe and record spontaneous interactions with the spout. Repeat the procedure for 15-minute sessions each day for three consecutive days.
Introduce the mice into the setup Gently place the mouse into the chamber to allow exploration. Let the animal acclimate to the environment. Ensure minimal handling to reduce stress
Remove the mouse and clean the setup

Prepare Setup Ensure the chamber is clean and free from any distractions. Position the water spout flush against the chamber wall (1 cm from the wall, 5.5 cm from the floor). Set up the camera in a lateral position to capture reaching movements. Include a reference marker in the video frame (e.g., a ruler or a known-distance object) to enable conversion from pixels to centimeters for kinematic analysis.

Prepare Setup Ensure the chamber is clean and free from any distractions. Position the water spout flush against the chamber wall (0 cm from the wall, 5.5 cm from the floor). Set up the camera in a lateral position to capture movements. Include a reference marker in the video frame (e.g., a ruler or a known-distance object) to enable conversion from pixels to centimeters for kinematic analysis.
Introduce the mice into the setup Gently place the mouse into the chamber. Ensure minimal handling to reduce stress

Start recording and water drop delivery Begin continuous video recording to monitor behavior. Automatically dispense a water drop at the spout. Observe and record spontaneous interactions with the spout. Repeat the procedure for 15-minute sessions each day for three consecutive days.
Remove the mouse and clean the setup
creator: Alessandro Contestabile
